reviews.mechanic: Drag the stuck 99% loading-bar handle to 100% to boot (fake-crash JUST KIDDING gag)
reviews.strengths: The drag-to-100% conceit is instantly legible: handle starts visibly at 99% with a pulsing "DRAG TO COMPLETE →" hint, right-only clamping prevents accidental regress, and the 20pt touch padding makes the small handle forgiving. The JUST KIDDING fake-crash payoff is a strong, on-theme first impression that earns the "GLITCHED" name.
reviews.concerns: No stuck-player escalation exists — there is no notePlayerStruggle/hintText or idle timer, so a confused player only ever gets the same looping hint. Both captured screenshots are still in the boot-log phase (pre-5.5s reveal), and the iPad image exposes a layout flaw: boot text is pinned to CGPoint(x:40, y:topSafeY-70) in the top-left, leaving the lower ~60% of the iPad an empty void with only the centered narrator line.
reviews.clueHint: Good t=0 framing: the log's final line "DRAG TO 100% TO BOOT" plus the on-screen "Loading.../99%/DRAG TO COMPLETE →" tells the player the action without ever hinting at the crash gag, so the JUST KIDDING twist stays a surprise. The escalation half fails — there is no inactivity detection or stronger hint if the player stalls.
reviews.suggestion: Add an idle-timeout escalation: if no drag is detected ~6s after revealMainUI, animate the handle with a nudging wiggle and swap the hint to something explicit like "← grab the white dot and slide it right", giving a stuck player real escalating help. Separately, on iPad anchor the boot text relative to scene-center (or center it) so it doesn't strand in the top-left of the wide canvas.

reviews.mechanic: Drag the LEVEL 1 HUD banner down into the spike pit to form a bridge
reviews.strengths: Strong teaching level: the 4th-wall conceit (the OS's own title becomes a physical bridge) is legible in both screenshots, and the un-jumpable 240pt pit (well past Bit's ~184pt reach) forces the intended mechanic instead of letting a jump bypass it. iPad re-composes into a full-height serpentine climb that fills the canvas with no void or overlap and stages the drag as a top-of-climb finale.
reviews.concerns: On iPhone the cyan t=0 whisper renders low-contrast on the white background and sits in the lower-center band right where Bit spawns, so it competes with the character for attention. The drop hit-test is very forgiving (any central-band drop near the pit bridges), which rescues accessibility/sim fallbacks but lets a player solve it without truly aiming, slightly diluting the "aha."
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well de-spoiled: a 2s-delayed whisper points at "the sign with my number on it" (the banner) without ever naming the drag gesture. A stuck player gets escalating help via hintText() — "Grab the LEVEL 1 banner and drag it straight down into the spike pit" — but only after notePlayerStruggle fires twice AND 8s elapsed, so it never pre-empts discovery.
reviews.suggestion: Boost the iPhone whisper's contrast/placement (move it above the spike pit or give it a dark backing plate) so it reads against the white floor and stops colliding with the spawning Bit; consider a brief one-time wiggle/pulse on the banner the first time the whisper fires to draw the eye to the draggable element without naming the gesture.

reviews.mechanic: Microphone (blow) — extend a wind-bridge across an un-jumpable chasm
reviews.strengths: The blow-to-extend mechanic is genuinely fair: the chasm is deliberately un-jumpable (235pt, explicitly widened to kill the running-jump bypass) so the bridge is unmistakably the only route, and the crossing is forgiving thanks to a boosted power curve plus asymmetric lerp (fast extend, slow 18pt/s retract) that leaves ample time to walk after one good breath. The wind theming (turbines, vents, hatched chasm, animated gust placard) sells the fiction before any text does.
reviews.concerns: The iPad render is the weak point: despite an elaborate isWideCanvas full-height tier-climb in source, the screenshot shows the flat phone layout pinned to the bottom quarter with the upper two-thirds an empty void — the composed climb isn't appearing, so the gap/overlap rules are moot and the screen reads unfinished. On phone the layout is clean and the chasm/bridge geometry is sound and completable.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-pitched: the persistent placard says only "LOOKS WINDY" with an animated gust glyph (diegetic, never names the mic), so the breath trick stays unspoiled. Escalation is real and tiered — death triggers notePlayerStruggle, then difficultyHintDidShow surfaces a "BLOW" mic placard with sound waves, and hintText() backs it with "Try blowing into the microphone."
reviews.suggestion: Fix/verify the iPad path so the composed tier-climb actually renders on portrait iPad (the screenshot still shows the bottom-anchored phone strip with a large empty band) — confirm the isWideCanvas gate trips on real device sizes, or fill the void with the intended ascending route so the chasm/bridge finale frames near the top instead of leaving two-thirds of the screen blank.

reviews.mechanic: Microphone (noise) — noise blocks lasers 1-3; the dashed 4th is inverse (silence = safe)
reviews.strengths: The inverse twist is taught beautifully in-world: the 4th laser is dashed, starts visibly OFF in silence, and wears a pulsing "THIS ONE LISTENS DIFFERENTLY." placard that flags the reversal without spelling out the answer. The noise-hold/decay fallback (1.4s floor at 0.45) makes the single-pulse accessibility path genuinely beatable while keeping silence reachable so #4 stays solvable.
reviews.concerns: iPad layout is the weak point: despite the code's "fills the full band top-to-bottom" claim, the entire playfield (Bit, platforms, all four lasers, exit door) sits in the bottom ~25% with a large empty TV-frame interior above it — a real vertical void. A player who blasts continuous noise to clear lasers 1-3 also arms the inverse 4th, so the cue load rests entirely on the placard/dashed style being noticed.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 panel says only "MAKE NOISE / TO BLOCK LASERS" — teaches the base rule, never spoils the inverse trick. Escalation is solid: notePlayerStruggle() fires on every death and hintText() now teaches BOTH halves ("noise ARMS it... go SILENT"), fixing the previously lethal hint.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the iPad vertical void: anchor the composed climb so the PEAK reaches the upper third of the TV interior (raise the fillTierCount band ceiling / lift the whole course) so the playfield occupies the screen instead of hugging the floor under a dead sky.

reviews.mechanic: Hardware volume buttons — lower the volume to keep the wolf asleep and the flood water low while walking to the exit.
reviews.strengths: Strong, legible single-screen staging: sleeping wolf with Z's, a dashed detection radius, and a live volume HUD make the cause-and-effect readable. The state machine (sleeping/stirring/awake/return-to-sleep) plus error haptic and the volume->water flood give the input two reinforcing consequences, and the de-spoiled poetic t=0 text fits the game's tone.
reviews.concerns: First-encounter fairness is the weak point: the input is a physical button with NO on-screen control, and the top-right volume widget is a passive readout that can read as decorative, so a new player may not connect "keep it quiet" to the hardware rocker. On iPad isWideCanvas is hardcoded false, so the whole hand-composed vertical-climb path is dead code and the level runs the flat phone band lifted to mid-screen — leaving a large empty void in the bottom ~40% (only faint waves) and a sparse top, so it does not fill the iPad screen.
reviews.clueHint: Yes on both counts but with a hole: t=0 ("IT SLEEPS. THE WATER LISTENS.") teaches the goal without naming the volume button, and notePlayerStruggle on each death escalates to hintText which explicitly says "Press your device's Volume-Down button." The gap: the escalation only fires on death (wolf/drown/fall), so a player who is simply stuck — standing still, never raising volume, never nearing the wolf — never triggers a struggle and never earns the reveal.
reviews.suggestion: Trigger the struggle/hint escalation on idle-stuck (no exit progress after ~15s), not only on death, so a passive stuck player still gets the volume-button reveal; and animate the volume HUD bars (a brief pulse/finger-tap cue at t=0) so it reads as the interactive control rather than a passive meter.

reviews.mechanic: Plug your real device into a charger; a giant plug bursts through the floor and elevators Bit up the shaft to the exit (.deviceCharging input -> triggerPlugAnimation, per-frame carry).
reviews.strengths: The carry/softlock engineering is genuinely robust: per-frame deltaY carry covers both the scripted rise and post-arrival sink, the respawn is relocated onto the exit platform after the floor breaks, plugMaxSink keeps a near-floor unplug recoverable, and the unplug/replug "COLD. SO COLD." loop is a nice fourth-wall beat. iPad layout fills the full canvas with no void.
reviews.concerns: The whole conceit is a fourth-wall "do something physical" surprise, yet a permanent on-screen label literally reads PLUG IN YOUR CHARGER, plus a pulsing empty-battery icon, so the trick is fully spoiled at t=0 with zero discovery moment. iPad-only adds a long gutter-climb/switchback that the iPhone never has, so the two devices play as different levels; the central vertical junction-box bead columns flanking the battery read as climbable but carry no physics.
reviews.clueHint: Fails the t=0 test: the literal solution (PLUG IN YOUR CHARGER) and hintText() both state the answer outright from frame one. There is no notePlayerStruggle override and no escalating reveal — help is binary and always-on rather than graduated.
reviews.suggestion: Stage the clue over time instead of spoiling it: at t=0 show only the dying/pulsing empty battery + a vague glitch taunt (e.g. "I'M... FADING"), and surface the explicit "Connect your device to a charger" string only after ~15-20s of inactivity via notePlayerStruggle/hintText, so a stuck player still gets the answer but a fresh player gets the aha.

reviews.mechanic: Raise device brightness to make UV "ghost" platforms solid (invisible <0.2, ghostly <0.5, fading-in <0.8, fully solid >=0.8); pushing to >=0.95 lights a sun/burn hazard that kills, so the goal is the high-but-not-max window.
reviews.strengths: The core loop is legible and self-teaching: platforms fade from dashed ghosts to glowing solids as brightness rises, and the on-screen LUX bar + indicator gives continuous physical feedback tied to the real hardware slider. The burn ceiling is genuinely well-staged — the safe band on the LUX bar stays hidden until the first burn (revealSweetSpotBand), turning the ceiling into an earned discovery rather than a printed rule. Jump budget is clean on both canvases (phone zig-zag ~16pt edge gaps / 58pt rises; iPad path explicitly capped at 130 e2e / 78 rise).
reviews.concerns: The two-line t=0 whisper on iPhone visibly overlaps the gameplay column ("THE DARK IS HIDING THE FLOOR FROM..." prints across the UV platforms and clips the canvas edge in the iPhone shot), undercutting the careful upper-band placement code — the wrap/centering looks off on this render. The mechanic relies on the player intuiting that the LUX bar maps to the OS brightness slider; nothing at t=0 connects "BRIGHTNESS / up-arrow" to the burn risk, so a first-timer may slam to 100% and eat a death before the system teaches the ceiling. iPad layout fills the full height well with no void/overlap.
reviews.clueHint: Yes on both counts: t=0 uses atmosphere ("THE DARK IS HIDING THE FLOOR FROM BOTH OF US") that frames the puzzle without naming brightness or the burn trick, and a stuck player escalates correctly — 2 deaths/struggles plus 8s of play fires hintText, which finally spells out "raise brightness... but pull back before full." Death-driven notePlayerStruggle plus progress-reset on crossing 0.6 is a sound escalation curve.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the iPhone t=0 caption so it never paints over the climb: the rendered text clips the right edge and sits on the platforms despite the upper-band logic — verify wrapTease against the actual compact width and consider lifting the band higher or shrinking font, since the de-spoil line currently reads worse than the iPad narrator path it was meant to improve on.

reviews.mechanic: Screenshot (Side + Vol-Up) freezes a flickering ghost bridge solid for a degrading window (5/3.5/2/1s) so Bit can cross an un-jumpable chasm.
reviews.strengths: The freeze-to-cross loop is legible and the geometry is honest: the 220pt chasm is correctly wider than Bit's ~184pt reach, so the bridge is genuinely required, and the degrading-freeze + screenshotCount-reset-on-death + 0.2s coyote grace make repeated attempts fair rather than punishing. The 4th-wall "that's in your camera roll forever" beat lands perfectly for the theme.
reviews.concerns: Two near-identical panels both lead with "SOME MOMENTS REFUSE TO MOVE..." (the t=0 discovery panel AND the persistent instruction panel) — redundant and it reads like a duplicate-render bug. On iPad the composed climb fills the floor well (no void/overlap at the platforms), but the entire upper-center canvas is dead white with a lone crosshair, and the finale chasm/exit scroll off-screen right, so at t=0 the player can't see where they're going. The mechanic also relies on a real OS gesture that fires an actual system screenshot each use — unavoidable side effect, but worth confirming it never gets debounced/swallowed by the screenshot UI.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-judged: "THE BRIDGE ONLY EXISTS WHEN NOTHING IS LOOKING. SO LOOK. / CATCH IT BEFORE IT FORGETS ITSELF." teases the freeze-the-moment idea without naming the screenshot gesture. Escalation is real: notePlayerStruggle() fires on every chasm death and hintText() at the base ~18s delay spells out "press Side + Volume Up," so a stuck player gets the explicit reveal.
reviews.suggestion: Cut the redundant second panel: keep the atmospheric t=0 discovery line OR the instruction panel, not both with the same opening sentence. And on iPad, either widen the camera or seed a faint "ghost preview" of the finale bridge so the empty mid-canvas isn't dead space at spawn.

reviews.mechanic: Toggle system dark/light to swap which ghost platforms are solid (moon=dark, sun=light); the exit door only unlocks in dark mode, while a shadow enemy makes lingering in dark on the light platform a hazard.
reviews.strengths: The "real platforms are a rumor in the other mode" 4th-wall conceit is genuinely clever and the dashed-ghost + moon/sun icons read clearly. Layout math is disciplined: an always-solid rest ledge guarantees Bit never stands on a vanishing platform, and the door multiplier was deliberately raised to 3.55 to close a rest->door bypass, so the required flip is truly un-skippable. Jump geometry is safe (rises ~25-57pt, well under 85; adjacent platforms overlap horizontally on iPhone so gaps are near-zero).
reviews.concerns: The core action is invisible: nothing on screen tells the player the trick is the OS Settings/Control-Center dark-mode switch, not an in-game button — and the prominent "TOGGLE DARK MODE" button + sun/moon pills only appear via the accessibility fallback (needsFallbackUI), so most players see a clue panel pointing at a control they may not realize is the system toggle. The iPad screenshot shows the upper ~60% of the canvas as empty void at this camera position with the climb hugging the bottom; the full-height composed climb only fills the screen as the camera pans, so the opening frame reads as a layout void. Tiny 7-8pt Menlo clue text is borderline illegible and partly occluded by the phone icon.
reviews.clueHint: Yes on both. The t=0 panel ("NIGHT AND DAY DON'T AGREE ON WHAT'S REAL... THE DOOR ONLY TRUSTS THE DARK") hints the day/night duality and dark-locked door without naming the action. Escalation is wired correctly: notePlayerStruggle() on each death progressively surfaces the explicit hintText ("Swipe down from the top-right and tap Dark Mode... the exit only unlocks in dark mode"), and notePlayerProgress() resets it once the player enters dark and unlocks the door.
reviews.suggestion: Make the system-toggle action discoverable without forcing a death-loop: after ~6-8s of no appearance change, auto-present a one-line GlitchedNarrator nudge that explicitly mimes the Control-Center swipe gesture (an animated finger/arrow from the top-right), so a first-timer learns the mechanic is the OS switch before frustration — distinct from the earned full hintText that still waits on real struggle.

reviews.mechanic: App backgrounding — leave the app ~5s+ so real elapsed time grows the sapling into a climbable tree-bridge to the exit (4 growth tiers; ancient tree at 10yrs/30s activates the exit).
reviews.strengths: Strong thematic cohesion: spinning clock dial, "0/10 YRS" tracker, growth sign and SYNCING flash all reinforce "time passes while you're away," and the 4th-wall return commentary ("YOU WERE GONE FOR N SECONDS. I COUNTED EVERY ONE.") gives the backgrounding trick real personality. The branch-ladder reach math is carefully verified (floor->first-foothold rise ~69pt, each branch +36..54pt, all <=80, head-squeeze overhang explicitly removed) so the post-growth climb is fair and completable.
reviews.clueHint: Good: t=0 panel "ALL THINGS TAKE THEIR TIME..." is evocative and does not spoil the background-the-app trick. Escalation works but only via the base class's 22s wall-clock no-progress fallback (lastProgressAt is set at play start) surfacing hintText() "Leave the app for a while — time passes"; this level never calls notePlayerStruggle(), and since there's no death/struggle on this puzzle, the faster 8s struggle path never triggers — the stuck player waits the full ~22s.
reviews.suggestion: Tighten the time-to-help loop and on-iPad framing: add a soft mid-tier nudge (e.g. fire notePlayerProgress-reset logic or a lighter pre-hint at ~12-15s of zero years) so a confused player isn't stranded for 22s, and on iPad bias the initial camera/spawn framing so the tree+cliff+exit cluster is the dominant on-screen element rather than the down-left staircase that points away from the goal.

reviews.mechanic: Rotate device to landscape to disarm a horizontally-creeping crusher and widen a too-narrow corridor (gap 18->100pt) so flat Bit can walk through to the exit. No jumps.
reviews.strengths: Strong "show don't tell" reveal: a looping 90-degree phone-rotation icon + circular arrow + top stretch-arrows + the 18pt gap vs 22pt Bit make the answer discoverable without naming the trick. The first-life 5s near-idle creep grace + 2s respawn grace turn what was a ~2.5s insta-kill into a fair, readable first encounter, and notePlayerStruggle escalates to an explicit hintText reveal after repeated death.
reviews.concerns: iPad portrait wastes a lot of canvas: the flat course is marooned in the lower-left and roughly the upper half plus right third read as empty grid despite the pillar/chevron framing, so it does not truly fill the screen (no overlap, but a visible void). On iPhone the crusher/skull is clipped off the left edge and two stacked top-center HUD plates crowd the header band. As a free-demo CLOSER this is the only level whose solution lives outside the touchscreen, so a player who never thinks to physically rotate can stall right at the paywall.
reviews.clueHint: Yes on both: t=0 lines ("UP IS A MATTER OF OPINION...", "THIS HALL WASN'T / BUILT FOR YOU.") tease hostile geometry without ever saying "rotate," and the animated rotation icon implies it visually; a stuck player gets notePlayerStruggle escalation per death plus the explicit hintText "Rotate the device to landscape... the corridor widens." Escalation is solid.
reviews.suggestion: Tighten the iPad framing: instead of the bottom-left pin, scale/center the flat course so the floor sits mid-screen and the camera band fills width edge-to-edge, and on iPhone shift the world right so the crusher/skull is fully on-screen (it is the visual that motivates fleeing right). Also consider auto-surfacing the rotate-icon pulse a few seconds in on the very first life so the demo-closer never dead-ends a player who hasn't realized the device itself is the input.

reviews.mechanic: Tap the pulsing REQUEST UNLOCK bell, wait, then tap the genuine GLITCHED push (not the spoofed SYSTEM decoy) to drop the door blocker; two sequential locked doors gate forward travel to the exit.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely smart escalation: a non-spoiling t=0 modal, a clear "TAP THE BELL BUTTON ABOVE / THEN WAIT" instruction panel, struggle/progress hooks, and a full hintText reveal. The decoy "SYSTEM" alert turns a one-tap ceremony into a real read-and-choose, and is fully recoverable. iPad reframes the flat phone course into a camera-followed vertical climb where door 0's unlock visibly drops a mid-ladder rung — the signature beat actually teaches the mechanic.
reviews.concerns: On iPad the bottom-left spawn area shows cluttered, overlapping geometry — the tier-0 pad, an L-shaped vertical block, and door 0's 115pt frame stack into a tangled intersection that reads as one ambiguous shape rather than "platform + locked door." The REQUEST UNLOCK bell sits pinned to top-center, far above the gameplay band on iPad, so the cause (bell) and effect (door at the bottom) are visually divorced. Phone layout and gaps are clean and within reach limits.
reviews.clueHint: Yes — the t=0 "EXTERNAL SIGNAL INTAKE" modal is abstract enough not to spoil the leave-the-app/tap-the-alert trick, while the instruction panel names the bell affordance without revealing the push step. A stuck player escalates correctly: notePlayerStruggle on death feeds the shared hint system, and hintText() ("Wait for the notification, then tap it to unlock the door") is the earned full reveal.
reviews.suggestion: Clean up the iPad bottom-left seam: nudge door 0 out of the spawn pad's footprint and remove the overlapping vertical block so platform and locked door read as distinct shapes, and add a faint visual link (e.g. a brief signal-line animation from the bell to door 0 on request) so the top-anchored bell and the bottom door are legibly connected.

reviews.mechanic: Copy the password GLITCH3D to the iOS clipboard outside the level, return, and tap PASTE PASSWORD on the terminal to unlock the locked door before the exit.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely inventive fourth-wall clipboard mechanic with a well-built un-jumpable locked-door trap (145pt door body puts the top ~16.5pt above Bit's ~91pt apex) and clean user-initiated paste that dodges the iOS paste prompt. iPhone layout is crisp: redacted password field, clear PASTE PASSWORD button, and an atmospheric two-line clue all legible.
reviews.concerns: The core fairness gap: the literal answer GLITCH3D appears ONLY in hintText() — it is redacted on the terminal and nothing in-scene copies it to the pasteboard, so a first-timer cannot know what to copy without the hint. On iPad the entire signature finale (terminal, PASTE control, redacted field, clue) is staged near the top tier and is OFF-SCREEN to the right at t=0; only blank platform stubs and the clipboard FAB are visible, so the puzzle reads as a staircase to nowhere until the camera scrolls.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 clue ("IT REMEMBERS WHAT YOU LAST HELD." / "...SOMEWHERE YOU ALREADY WERE.") is nicely atmospheric and does not spoil. Escalation exists via notePlayerStruggle()/notePlayerProgress()/hintText(), but struggle only increments on death — a player stuck by confusion (standing, not dying) never triggers escalation, the exact failure mode this clipboard puzzle produces.
reviews.suggestion: Make the answer discoverable in-world and tie escalation to confusion, not just death: render the literal string GLITCH3D somewhere copyable in-scene (e.g. a "select to copy" glyph/log line near the terminal, or seed it onto the pasteboard with a visible "COPIED: GLITCH3D" toast) so the puzzle is solvable without reading hintText, and add a timed/idle notePlayerStruggle() trigger so a stuck-but-not-dying player still earns the escalating hint.

reviews.mechanic: Toggle WiFi to solidify signal platforms / pass walls
reviews.strengths: The two-segment design genuinely forces both states: WiFi-ON stepping stones are the only bridge across the 232-logical chasm, then a too-tall wall plus an OFF-only climb step force exactly one OFF toggle, so the mechanic can't be brute-forced by jumping. The on-screen blue/slashed toggle buttons, signal-bar HUD, download bar, and the wall's antenna texture all reinforce the "signal = solid" read, and toggles only ever happen while Bit stands on a WiFi-independent rest ledge, so nothing de-solidifies underfoot on the intended path. The iPad climb is a real ascending route that fills the canvas with the trap staged high, not a centered phone course in dead sky.
reviews.concerns: Visual legibility of the load-bearing pieces is weak: the WiFi-ON stepping stones render as barely-visible 0.3-alpha ghosts and the OFF-step is a faint gray outline, so on first encounter a player can't tell the stones are platforms vs decoration. The WiFi-OFF step's antenna+slash icon is tiny and easy to miss, so the second half ("go OFF to climb") is far less self-evident than the first. On iPad the finale trap and its stones sit off-screen to the right until the camera scrolls, so the signature puzzle isn't previewed. Jump-reach math checks out (all sub-hops and the +55/+45 stairs stay within the ~91pt apex and gap<=130 limits).
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is good: "THE SIGNAL COMES AND GOES..." plus the narrator asides name the theme without revealing the ON-to-cross / OFF-to-climb sequence. But escalation is thin: the only fallback is a single static hintText ("Toggle WiFi in Control Center"), there's no notePlayerStruggle hook, and the hint points to Control Center even though on-screen toggle buttons exist, which can mislead a stuck player.
reviews.suggestion: Make the state-dependent surfaces unmistakable: render WiFi-ON stones and the OFF-step at ~0.55-0.6 alpha with a dashed/ghosted fill (not 0.3 near-invisible) and enlarge their icons, and add an escalating hint via notePlayerStruggle (e.g. after N deaths on the chasm: "Signal builds the bridge"; after stalling at the wall: "Drop the signal to climb past the wall") instead of the single Control-Center line.

reviews.mechanic: Enable iOS Focus / Do Not Disturb to freeze orbiting/oscillating spikes and latch the moon-locked exit open, then walk Bit out through the now-still hazards.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely clever fourth-wall hook: the real OS Focus toggle is the mechanic, reinforced by a moon-lock door, calming white wash, and a "DO NOT DISTURB? I AM THE DISTURBANCE" narrator sting. iPhone layout is clean and the latch (hasFocusedOnce) means players cannot soft-lock by toggling Focus off after unlocking. Hazards correctly hover over jump arcs, not landing spots.
reviews.concerns: iPad is the main problem: the screenshot shows the course hugging the bottom-right quadrant with the entire upper-left ~60% as dead void, so the "full vertical band" claim in createHazardsComposedIPad fails visually. The two HUD moon circles (bottom-left) are ambiguous — purple/gray with no label, players will not read them as a manual Focus toggle, and they duplicate the clearer "CAN'T DO THIS?" button. The core trick is also discoverable only if a player already knows iOS Focus exists; a stuck novice may never think to leave the app.
reviews.clueHint: Good: t=0 panel ("THE NOISE NEVER STOPS... UNLESS YOU LET IT. SO HOW QUIET CAN YOU MAKE YOURSELF?") teases the silence theme without naming Focus. Escalation works — notePlayerStruggle() fires on each death, presentLockedExitCue() shows "FOCUS TO OPEN" + a moon-lock pulse on door contact, and hintText() finally spells out the Control Center crescent-moon toggle plus the CAN'T DO THIS? fallback.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the iPad vertical fill so the composed climb actually spans top-to-bottom (the screenshot proves it does not — anchor/verify composedCeilingY against the real visible camera viewport, or lift the whole band), and label/consolidate the two bare moon circles into one clearly-titled manual Focus toggle instead of relying on the unlabeled purple/gray dots.

reviews.mechanic: Low Power toggle swaps normal/lunar gravity per section
reviews.strengths: Genuinely clever bidirectional gravity puzzle: the narrow drop is a normal-gravity gate while the wide chasm is a height-gate that only the lunar arc clears, so the player must toggle the POWER button BOTH ways. The iPad path replaces the old left-right course with a confined floor-to-ceiling zig-zag column that fills the frame with no void or overlap, and the apex math is documented and device-independent.
reviews.concerns: The on-playfield link between the "POWER ON/OFF" button and gravity is never stated at t=0, and "Low Power" never appears in-scene, so a first-timer has no signal that the bottom-right button flips physics. The bidirectional requirement (OFF for the drop, ON for the chasm) is the hardest idea and gets zero teaching until the struggle-gated hint. On iPhone there is a tall dead-white band between the instruction panel and the gameplay, and the two yellow/green battery pills bottom-left read as ambiguous extra UI next to the POWER button.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-judged and unspoiled: "THE BATTERY IS DYING / EVERYTHING GETS LIGHTER WHEN I LET GO" is atmospheric and never names the button or the toggle trick. Escalation is solid and wired correctly — notePlayerStruggle fires per death, notePlayerProgress resets on the first low-power engage, and hintText spells out both directions of the POWER button precisely once a player is stuck.
reviews.suggestion: Add a one-time diegetic cue the first time Bit walks near the POWER button (or on the chasm's first death) such as a small "tap to throttle power" label or a brief gravity-arrow flash, so the button-to-gravity link is discoverable without spoiling the bidirectional reversal, and clarify/remove the two battery pills so the only obvious control is POWER.

reviews.mechanic: Shake to rewind 3s (limited charges)
reviews.strengths: Genuinely required mechanic: the forced rotten final platform collapses and strands Bit on a catch ledge with the exit ~128pt up (past the ~91pt apex), so shake-to-undo is load-bearing, not cosmetic. Completability is rigorously guarded — the trap-repair undo is free, undoCount is floored to 1 (softlock guard), the inert exit gates behind repair, and preTrapAnchor gives a safe rewind even on a late undo. iPhone layout is clean and well-filled.
reviews.concerns: iPad layout has a large dead void: the composed full-height climb renders crammed into the bottom ~40% with the entire top half (below the instruction panel to ~y=1100) empty white space — the ipadCeilingY = topSafeY-150 / tier math is not lifting the route high enough, so the "dead-sky fix" it documents is partially unmet here. The mechanic verb is never reachable through escalating help from a stuck-on-the-ledge player except via the 1.25s JIT prompt: the non-lethal trap means notePlayerStruggle (death-gated) never fires while stranded, so the JIT prompt is the sole on-ledge nudge — fine if it always fires, but it is the only line of defense.
reviews.clueHint: Yes — t=0 panel ("REGRET HAS A GRIP. USE IT / MISTAKES CAN BE UNMADE / BUT NOT FOREVER") is atmospheric and never names "shake," preserving the discovery. A stuck player gets strong escalation: a 1.25s post-collapse "SHAKE TO UNDO" JIT prompt (VoiceOver-spoken), plus the explicit death-gated hintText() naming the gesture and the top-left undo counter.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the iPad vertical fill: raise ipadCeilingY (it is anchored too low relative to the actual rendered top) and/or verify ipadFillTierCount is producing enough tiers so the PEAK/FINALE land near the screen top, eliminating the empty upper half seen in the iPad capture. Re-screenshot to confirm the climb spans floor-to-ceiling like the iPhone path does.

reviews.mechanic: Toggle the device's real Airplane Mode ON to raise flying platforms from their death-plane "landed" positions up to a walkable climb; OFF drops them back into the hazard.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely clever fourth-wall mechanic: the flying platforms literally live in the death plane until you flip a hardware toggle, so the puzzle and the theme are one and the same. The per-platform protection (flyingPlatformSupportingBit) plus the reachability-blip debounce shows real care — toggling OFF while standing on a flyer won't yank you into the hazard, and a spurious NWPathMonitor OFF can't kill a stationary player. iPad path is a thoughtfully paced 13-tier climb with named beats (teach/stair/rest/tension/finale) and camera-follow.
reviews.concerns: iPad first paint reads as a void: with mode OFF only the 4 solids exist on-screen and the spawn floor (x110) sits bottom-left while the next solid (REST, x705/tier4) is far-right mid-frame, leaving a huge empty middle band and a lone orphan platform outline — the 12 flyers are all hidden in the death plane below, so the screen looks broken/incomplete until the player toggles. On iPhone the panel's right edge crowds under the glowing pause button. The spawn-to-first-flyer horizontal step is 125pt (gap<=130) and last-flyer-to-finale-solid is 115pt — both legal but near the ceiling, so any turbulence X-wobble (±1.5) plus Bit's run momentum makes the upper finale jumps feel unforgiving.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-handled: three atmospheric lines ("CUT THE WORLD LOOSE. LET IT RISE.") evoke flight/tethering without ever naming Airplane Mode, so the trick stays unspoiled. Escalation is solid — notePlayerStruggle() fires on each death and hintText() then spells out "Turn ON Airplane Mode — tap the plane icon or swipe Control Center down," giving a stuck player the explicit how-to only after repeated failure.
reviews.suggestion: Show a faint, dashed "ghost" silhouette of each flying platform at its ON/flying target Y while mode is OFF (on both layouts, but critical for iPad). This fills the empty void on first paint, telegraphs that something will rise into that space, and makes the puzzle's intent legible the instant the level loads — turning the current "is this screen broken?" iPad moment into a deliberate "ah, those will fly up" reveal.

reviews.mechanic: Peek the app switcher (swipe up to background/peek) to freeze hazards and reveal dotted spike trajectories, giving you time to plan the jump.
reviews.strengths: Strong concept that fuses the fourth-wall narrator ("DON'T YOU DARE SWITCH") with a genuinely un-eye-timeable hazard (desynced, per-leg jitter oscillation) so the freeze is mechanically necessary, not cosmetic. Trajectory lines + physicsWorld.speed=0 + decaying peek budget (5.0->1.5s) is a clean, fair loop, and the phone layout reads cleanly with safe ~25pt gaps / 30pt rises.
reviews.concerns: The iPad layout is broken: the diagonal staircase climbs from bottom-left to top-right but the camera follows X only with Y pinned to scene center, so the spawn/start and the instruction panel sit in different vertical zones, the top tiers (and the exit) scroll into screen with huge dead whitespace, and the spike hover offsets (80-140pt above each beat) put the finale crossfire far above its platform. The trajectory lines are drawn as scene children (addChild) while hazards/platforms also live in the scene but the camera scrolls, so on iPad lines render correctly only because everything is scene-space, yet the freeze overlay/timer are camera-parented at center, leaving the timer visually detached from the action low on screen.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 panel ("THE WORLD HOLDS ITS BREATH WHEN YOU LOOK AWAY / SO. STOP WATCHING.") nicely hints "look away = freeze" without naming the App Switcher, so it teases without spoiling. Escalation exists: handleDeath calls notePlayerStruggle and hintText() is explicit ("Swipe up slightly... the spikes' paths draw themselves"), and enterPeekMode calls notePlayerProgress to reset the timer on success.
reviews.suggestion: For iPad, drop the diagonal-climb redesign and instead scale/center the proven phone course as a single horizontal band (like the phone path's courseX centering) at the vertical mid-screen, OR add vertical camera-follow that tracks Bit's Y so the climb, exit, and freeze timer stay co-located. The current X-only camera over a full-height diagonal is the root cause of the void/detached-HUD issues.

reviews.mechanic: Authenticate (Face ID / proximity) to clear two sequential biometric vault gates; the exit body is trapped behind door2's blocker so the second scan is mandatory.
reviews.strengths: The two-stage "FACE CHANGED - RESCANNING" escalation and the trap where the exit hides behind door2's blocker is a genuinely clever, well-coupled twist, and the imposter alarm (red flash + door shake + error haptic, with a Reduce-Motion soft-fade) sells the fiction. The decline-count software fallback (declinesBeforeFallback=2 -> forceHardwareFallback) is excellent: it keeps biometrics as the real gate yet guarantees no hard softlock for a player who cancels Face ID.
reviews.concerns: iPad layout is the weak point: the screenshot shows gameplay crammed into the bottom ~40% with a large empty void between the centered modal and the staircase, and door1/the finale are scrolled off the right edge - the "fills full iPad height" claim isn't borne out because the camera Y stays centered on a low-spawned Bit. Mechanic clarity also lags: t=0 only mentions one vault, so the existence of a SECOND sequential gate (and that the exit is trapped behind it) is undiscoverable until after step 1, when "TAP THE NEXT GATE" first appears.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-judged: "TAP IT TO SCAN YOUR FACE" teaches the verb without spoiling the multi-scan twist. A stuck player is covered by the base wall-clock no-progress net (~22s) surfacing hintText() "Authenticate identity"; status labels ("ONE MORE SCAN", "TAP THE NEXT GATE", "USE ON-SCREEN ID BUTTON") escalate well, but the level never calls notePlayerStruggle(), so it leans entirely on the generic fallback rather than a faster death/struggle-driven hint.
reviews.suggestion: On the iPad composed path, raise the follow-camera so the centered modal sits above the gameplay band (or lower the spawn band) to kill the mid-screen void, and add an early on-screen marker/arrow toward door2 once door1 opens so the two-gate structure is legible before the player walks into the trapped exit.

reviews.mechanic: Walk Bit INTO the corruption wall to trigger a (simulated) system purge that clears the blocks and opens the exit.
reviews.strengths: Robust dual trigger (physics contact in didBegin + proximity-0.6 fallback) makes the purge fire reliably; the original "delete the app / fake review" gimmick was wisely converted to a self-contained simulated purge that is App Store safe. iPad is a genuine bottom-to-top climb with camera-follow and anchored HUD, and all gaps (≤130) / rises (≤85) sit inside Bit's budget. Strong A11Y: announceObjective + Reduce-Motion guards on the block flicker/glitch.
reviews.concerns: NOTE: both screenshots caught the ~6.5s ominous-intro flicker ("RECOMMEND: FULL SYSTEM PURGE") over black, so the playable course is not visible in either capture — layout verified from source, not pixels. The escalating-help path is death-driven (notePlayerStruggle fires only on handleDeath), but this puzzle's real failure mode is inaction: the phone path is a flat overlapping floor with no hazard before the wall, so a player who just stops at the wall face never dies and never unlocks hintText. The post-purge exit gap is exactly 130 (at the cap, zero margin).
reviews.clueHint: Yes — the t=0 panel ("CORRUPTED DATA BLOCKS THE EXIT / IT WANTS YOU TO TURN BACK. EVERYTHING DOES.") frames the wall as something to confront without spoiling that you walk into it. hintText() gives a clean explicit reveal, but it only escalates on death, which this hazard-free approach rarely produces.
reviews.suggestion: Decouple the escalating hint from death: add a stall/idle timer (e.g., Bit pressed against the wall face or no forward progress for ~8-10s) that calls notePlayerStruggle(), so a confused-but-alive player who stops short of the wall still earns the "push INTO the blocks" hint.

reviews.mechanic: Name-door: the OS reads your device name, greets you by it, and the exit is the one door (of three, flanked by GUEST/ADMIN decoys) labeled with your name; a doppelganger NPC races you and dies at a decoy.
reviews.strengths: Strong fourth-wall hook — being addressed by your real device name on a labeled door lands. iPad path is excellent: the centered low band is replaced by a hand-paced full-height zig-zag climb with the door cluster at the ceiling, so there is no void and the screen fills top-to-bottom. Bay pillars cleverly force a real choice instead of a single rightward stroll.
reviews.concerns: Once names resolve the "puzzle" is trivial and consequence-free — wrong doors are just inert, so you walk to your name with no failure cost, making the doppelganger race largely decorative/redundant with the labels. On iPhone the t=0 narrator line "I KNOW WHO YOU ARE..." renders mid-band and overlaps the astronaut and platforms (legible but cluttered); doors show only "?" for the first ~5s until the name event/fallback fires, which can read as a stall. Jump-reach is fine (all gaps ~5-20pt, single 30pt rise, far inside 130/85).
reviews.clueHint: Yes — t=0 "FIND THE DOOR WITH YOUR NAME" + "THE DOOR KNOWS TOO" points at the name mechanic without spoiling the doppelganger trick; and notePlayerStruggle()/hintText() surfaces "Your device knows your name..." after repeated deaths, so a stuck player gets escalating, non-spoiling help.
reviews.suggestion: Give the wrong doors a real consequence so the choice has stakes: make a decoy door visibly "reject"/flash-and-respawn (or briefly lock) on contact, and arm the doppelganger to actually beat you to the exit if you dawdle — turn the labeled-name read into a timed decision rather than a labeled walk.

reviews.mechanic: Speak BRIDGE / OPEN / FLY (in order) to extend a bridge over an un-jumpable chasm, unlock a door, then briefly fly to the exit ledge.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely novel voice mechanic with three obstacles each labeled by the exact word above it; FLY is correctly gated behind BRIDGE+OPEN with an in-scene correction, so the ordered solution can't be short-circuited. Strong accessibility: working no-mic button fallback plus the universal hatch.
reviews.concerns: The transient timing windows (5s bridge retract, 2s FLY) aren't telegraphed at t=0, so a first-timer who walks too slowly after BRIDGE can get silently stranded mid-chasm and not understand why. iPad tier step is ~83pt against the 85 rise cap — safe but only ~2pt margin. Finale is off-screen right on the iPad (camera-follow), so the screenshot only shows the climb.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 is well-judged: the panel says only "SAY THE WORD" while the specific commands live on-course at each obstacle, so the trick isn't spoiled up front. Escalation is real — each death calls notePlayerStruggle() and hintText() eventually reveals the full ordered solution (BRIDGE, then OPEN, then FLY last).
reviews.suggestion: Surface the bridge's 5s retract timer visually (e.g., the bridge alpha fading out as it nears retraction, or a brief "BRIDGE HOLDING…" countdown), so a player who hesitates mid-chasm understands the failure instead of dying to an invisible clock.

reviews.mechanic: Battery % controls how many stones exist; the real exit is hidden below the 50% stone and only unlocks when battery drops under 60% (so you must DRAIN power, not keep it).
reviews.strengths: The counterintuitive "drain to win" trick is genuinely clever and is implemented airtight: the hidden exit body starts inert (category .none) and only becomes a real .exit at <60%, so it can't be cheesed at full battery. The DRAIN POWER fallback makes it playable in sim, dim-overlay + atmosphere mood shift give satisfying drain feedback, and the iPad climb maps % to altitude so draining visibly drops the top of the course away.
reviews.concerns: First-encounter fairness is shaky: nothing tells the player the DRAIN POWER button is the answer, so the intended insight (lower battery to reveal the exit) is easy to miss while they bounce off the fake EXIT? trap. On iPad the 40% stone's right edge (~612) overlaps the 50% plateau's left edge (~570), a ~43pt overlap, and a wide unlabeled connective platform sits ambiguously next to the plateau; vertical fill is otherwise good (no void). Reach is fine — start->10% gap ~50pt, tier rises sized by fillTierCount so each +1 step stays <=85.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 text ("SOME THINGS ONLY APPEAR WHEN YOU'RE RUNNING LOW / MORE POWER ISN'T ALWAYS BETTER") telegraphs the inversion without naming battery or the drain action, which is the right amount of misdirection. Escalation works via notePlayerStruggle()->hintText "Lower your battery % — the real way down only appears when power drops," but it's death-gated, so a player who merely wanders or only hits the fake exit may never trigger it.
reviews.suggestion: Make struggle detection time/idle-based as well as death-based (e.g. start the hint timer after N seconds with battery still >=60% or after the player has touched the fake exit twice), so a stuck-but-not-dying player still gets escalated toward DRAIN POWER. Separately, nudge the 40% stone left or the 50% plateau right to remove the ~43pt iPad overlap.

reviews.mechanic: Clear cache to dissolve a DATA MASS wall (reach + arm a PURGE TERMINAL on a ledge, then free storage / "CAN'T DO THIS?" to purge the unjumpable junk wall blocking the exit).
reviews.strengths: The arm-the-terminal gating is a genuinely strong redesign: it kills the old one-tap trivia by requiring a short jump-up traverse before any purge counts, and the cascade-by-stratum dissolve sells "clutter being reclaimed." On iPhone the layout is clean and legible: junk wall, terminal, asides, and EXIT all read clearly, and the panels sit safely clear of the title/pause bands.
reviews.concerns: The iPad render directly contradicts the code's own "VOID FIX" comments: instead of a confined vertical zig-zag column centered on size.width/2, the platforms march as a diagonal staircase up-and-right while the spawn sits bottom-left, leaving the entire upper ~50% and center-right of the frame empty white void. Worse, the terminal ledge, the JUNK MASS wall, and the EXIT door are all absent from the visible iPad frame — so the mechanic's payoff and goal are invisible, and the level reads as incomplete/unfair on iPad first encounter. iPhone is fine; the wide-canvas composition is the failure.
reviews.clueHint: Good on both axes. The t=0 intro panel ("JUNK MASS WALLS OFF THE EXIT / JUMP TO THE PURGE TERMINAL FIRST") states the obstacle and the first step without revealing the device cache-clear trick, and the in-world aside reinforces "reach the terminal, then make me let go." Escalation is layered: pre-arm hintText points to the ledge, a stray purge before arming triggers a warning haptic + terminal flash, and only after arming does the concrete "FREE UP STORAGE / CAN'T DO THIS?" instruction surface.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the iPad composition so it actually does what the comments claim: clamp the climb into a real vertical column hugging size.width/2 (verify ipadClimbCenterX/offset math — it is rendering as a wide diagonal, not a confined ±48 column), and guarantee the terminal ledge near the ceiling, the full-height junk wall, and the EXIT are all on-screen in the single resting frame. Add an automated wide-canvas audit asserting junk-wall bounds, terminal ledge, and exit door are within the visible viewport (since the camera no longer follows) so this void can't regress silently.

reviews.mechanic: Real-clock time-of-day: NIGHT sleeps the patrolling spike guarding the only safe landing; CYCLE TIME button overrides day/night/secret(3:33) on demand.
reviews.strengths: The required-mechanic loop is genuinely sound: the DAY guard sweeps P2's only safe landing so the player must switch to NIGHT to sleep it, and the override button decouples the puzzle from the real clock so it is always solvable. Geometry checks out on phone (P1->P2 open gap 130 <= reach, all rises <= ~13pt) and the secret 3:33 variant is a nice meta payoff.
reviews.concerns: On the iPhone shot the two guard spikes sit on the LEFT shoulder of the wide P2/exit pad rather than centered over the forced landing zone, so a player may read "land right of the spikes" and never feel forced to use NIGHT (the puzzle risks reading as a timing/positioning dodge rather than a required toggle). The P1->P2 single jump is also the only real challenge, so the level is very short. iPad layout fills the full height correctly with no void/overlap and the HUD (time/cycle button) is camera-pinned.
reviews.clueHint: Good: the t=0 panel ("THE WORLD CHANGES WITH THE CLOCK" / "SOMETHING GUARDS THAT LEDGE. IT WON'T ALWAYS BE WATCHING.") points at the day/night idea without naming NIGHT or the button, so the trick is not spoiled. Escalation also works: handleDeath() calls notePlayerStruggle() and hintText() explicitly says tap CYCLE TIME until it reads NIGHT, so repeated deaths surface real help.
reviews.suggestion: Re-center the guard's patrol so its sweep fully covers P2's ONLY safe landing band (extend enemy 0 range or shift center so the awake DAY spike makes every viable landing lethal), and make the spike visually overlap the platform top edge so a first-time player clearly cannot land there in DAY. This converts the puzzle from a possible timing-dodge into an unambiguous "must sleep it" gate.

reviews.mechanic: Change the device's system language to unscramble the signs and swap the dead-end route for the real one to the exit.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely novel fourth-wall hook with a tasteful, non-spoiling t=0 panel ("not in a tongue this device still remembers") plus tri-lingual revert steps. The phone column is tight and readable, and the locale latch/baseline-relative gate logic is carefully thought through.
reviews.concerns: On iPad the correct staircase is alpha=0/non-solid until solved, so the t=0 screenshot shows a huge dead void through the vertical center and an isolated exit plateau top-right — the "vertical fill" is invisible and the screen reads as broken. The leap from "scrambled signs" to "open iOS Settings and change the whole phone language" is large and unguided on first encounter.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 panel hints at language without naming the Settings trick, so it doesn't spoil it; hintText() gives the exact Settings path. But escalation is wired only to deaths (notePlayerStruggle on handleDeath), and a stuck player stands safely on the rest pad without dying, so the most likely stuck state never triggers help.
reviews.suggestion: Give the hidden iPad staircase a faint low-alpha (still non-solid) ghost outline so the band reads as intentional and a route is visibly waiting to be revealed, and add a time-on-level trigger to notePlayerStruggle so a stuck-but-alive player still gets escalating hints.

reviews.mechanic: Invisible-but-solid stepping stones across a void; toggling VoiceOver (or the on-screen fallback) phases the path in, real stones gain collision while barred decoys reveal-but-stay-intangible.
reviews.strengths: The rework is genuinely smart: it makes VoiceOver STATE (not live input) the trigger, sidestepping the fatal flaw that VoiceOver hijacks single-finger touches, and latches the path so you platform normally after revealing. The decoy "SOLID BEARS WEIGHT / BARRED LIES" twist adds real puzzle depth, and an always-present on-screen fallback means it never requires actually enabling VoiceOver. iPhone reach math is clean (226pt span, ~49pt gaps, ~40pt first rise — all well within budget).
reviews.concerns: iPad is the weak point: the climb is a ~250pt-wide centered zig-zag column on a 1024+pt canvas, so the screenshot shows huge blank margins left and right — vertically full, horizontally a thin ribbon in an ocean of white, reading as underfilled/void. Mechanic clarity at t=0 is fine (gap genuinely empty), but a brand-new player has no in-world cue that the fix is "engage accessibility" until death #2; the phrase "phase the path in" is jargon a first-timer may not map to the purple a11y button.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 panel is thematic and non-spoiling ("something here is listening for you / a path exists only once it's named aloud") — never says VoiceOver outright, good. Escalation is solid: deathCount>=2 splits between "PHASE THE PATH IN FIRST" (not yet revealed) vs flashing barred decoys (revealed but stepping wrong), plus notePlayerStruggle->hintText() gives an explicit Settings/accessibility-button instruction.
reviews.suggestion: Widen the iPad column to fill the canvas: keep the vertical tier climb but author the zig-zag dx at roughly +/-200-260 (instead of +/-58) and let the REST/BREATH platforms span wider, so the route uses the full iPad width and the blank side-margins disappear; verify edge-to-edge gaps stay <=130 at the larger offsets (or re-enable camera-follow for a wider course).

reviews.mechanic: Share a scrambled 6-symbol transmission through the system share sheet (AirDrop/Messages/Notes) to "decode" it (payload is the plaintext), then type the 6 symbols back on a decoy-salted keypad to unlock an un-jumpable door.
reviews.strengths: Genuinely clever diegetic decode: sending the cipher out of the app and reading the plaintext back is a real, novel use of the share sheet, and the salted keypad means you must actually know the answer rather than mash keys. iPhone terminal layout is clean and self-explanatory, and the door-height gate (120pt body vs ~91pt apex) correctly forces the puzzle.
reviews.concerns: The core loop depends on the OS share sheet round-trip, which is fragile/opaque on first encounter — a player can complete the share without understanding that the *received text* is the answer, especially if they share to Notes and don't switch apps. The visible "RULE: ROTATE BACK 5" plus the 6-char cipher invites players to try decoding in-head, but the keypad's symbol set is restricted, so manual rotation may not even be tappable, which is confusing. On iPad the finale (terminal + door) is fully off-screen at spawn; the screenshot is a long empty staircase with no visible goal, so the signature mechanic isn't previewed at all.
reviews.clueHint: Good: t=0 ("THE CODE IS SCRAMBLED / I CAN'T READ MYSELF IN HERE / TAKE ME SOMEWHERE I CAN") nudges toward sharing without naming AirDrop or spoiling that the payload is the plaintext. Escalation is solid: hintText() gives a full step-by-step, notePlayerStruggle() fires on wrong submits AND deaths, and the "CAN'T DO THIS?" fallback reveals the decoded code on the terminal yet still requires typing it.
reviews.suggestion: On iPad, anchor a small persistent "TRANSMISSION AHEAD →" arrow or a camera-pinned mini-preview of the locked door so the scrolled finale is telegraphed from spawn; the empty staircase currently hides the entire mechanic. Secondarily, drop or de-emphasize the literal "RULE: ROTATE BACK 5" line since it baits unsolvable in-head decoding and undercuts the share gimmick.

reviews.mechanic: "No gimmick, just walk" lie: you traverse right to a fake EXIT, the OS glitches and reveals the real exit was hidden back at your spawn — walk left to win.
reviews.strengths: The twist is genuinely well-built: the t=0 panel and subtitle sell the lie without leaking it, the reveal sequence (boss-register narrator, door shake, "GO BACK" arrow + chevron breadcrumb pinned left, fade-in real door) clearly redirects the player, and the player-analysis/trust beat ties the hesitation tracking back into the theme. iPad gets a real full-height diagonal staircase that fills the canvas top-to-bottom with no void.
reviews.concerns: iPhone reach is safe — platforms 80..920 then lastPlatformX have edge-to-edge gaps ~30-90pt and rises <=30pt (well under 130/85); iPad climbs one tier/beat so each rise is <=85 by construction. iPad's per-tier overlap on tall devices only shrinks gaps. Main worry is the long, empty, breadcrumb-free walk-BACK from fake exit (~fakeExitX) all the way to spawn x=80: with platforms only "shaken" (not removed), the return trip is uneventful and could read as "is this broken?" before the real door scrolls into view.
reviews.clueHint: Yes — t=0 ("NO GIMMICK. JUST WALK") is the bait and never hints the exit is behind you, preserving the trick. Escalation works: deaths call notePlayerStruggle() and the earned hintText() asks "Are you sure the exit is ahead?" — a nudge toward the twist that still doesn't spoil it; revealTruth() calls notePlayerProgress() to reset the counter.
reviews.suggestion: Add a single mid-course breadcrumb on the walk-back — e.g. a faint "<<" marker (or briefly re-light the real-exit door's glow) roughly halfway between fakeExitX and spawn — so the return trip has a confirming waypoint and never feels like a dead/empty corridor before the camera reaches x=80.

reviews.mechanic: Climb a vertical zigzag ladder of bright credit "platforms" to a star exit door, dodging scurrying bug hazards; the credits roll is a deliberate fake-out ending before World 5 rips it back.
reviews.strengths: The fake-out concept is genuinely charming and the platforms-as-credits gag reads instantly. Jump-reach is provably safe on both devices: phone rise 76 < 85 cap and zigzag center gaps of 60 < 130; iPad rises are verticalTier-clamped to <=85 and halfSpread is capped at 120 < 130, so every hop is reachable. Bugs got a white-fill legibility pass so they actually read as hazards against the black backdrop.
reviews.concerns: t=0 instruction-panel overlap is the headline bug. On iPhone the camera-anchored panel collides with the "LEVEL 30" title's "30" glyph and its right edge clips the pause-button chevron. On iPad it's worse: the centered panel sits on the top finale beat, with "FOR PLAYING" peeking above it and a "YOU" credit box bleeding out below, and the star exit door floats just over it -- the very top of the climb is half-hidden behind the HUD card. On both devices the astronaut spawns overlapping the "GLITCHED PRODUCTION / THE FINAL LEVEL" boxes, mangling that text.
reviews.clueHint: Excellent. The t=0 panel ("CLIMB THE CREDITS / WATCH OUT FOR BUGS") frames it as an ordinary level and never tips the fake-out, preserving the twist. Escalation is wired: handleDeath() calls notePlayerStruggle() and hintText() returns "Climb the credits -- watch for bugs!" -- same non-spoiler info on repeat death.
reviews.suggestion: Fix the t=0 panel placement: on iPad the centered panel must not land on the finale tier/exit door -- offset it into a genuinely empty band (or move it to the lower spawn area near Bit), and on iPhone push it clear of both the "LEVEL 30" title row and the pause column. Separately, nudge Bit's spawn 20-30pt so it doesn't overlap the TEACH platform's text. Both are pure layout offsets with no mechanic risk.

reviews.mechanic: Flashlight + device-tilt to reveal a dark cave: an SKCropNode masks all level content to an ellipse beam; held vertical the beam throws long/forward to spot ceiling stalactites, held flat it pools at the feet to catch pit gaps.
reviews.strengths: The crop-mask beam that morphs shape with pitch (vertical=long forward throw, flat=floor pool) is a genuinely clever, on-theme use of hardware, and the always-on ambient glow + faintly pulsing exit glow are smart fail-safes so you can never be fully blind. iPhone gaps all sit safely (largest Section 3/4 gap ~60pt vs 130 cap) and Section 4 stalactites were deliberately raised to gapFromFloor 175 to clear the jump apex, so it is completable. The de-spoiled t=0 tease and the strong escalating-hint chain are the high points.
reviews.concerns: Discovery of the core trick is gated behind 2 deaths + 8s, a long blind window; on iPad the unlit canvas reads as an empty void because silhouette hints sit at ~0.04 alpha. Geometry/completability are solid on both form factors.
reviews.clueHint: Good on both axes: t=0 panel ("IT'S DARK DOWN HERE. I CAN'T SEE A THING FROM IN HERE — CAN YOU?") teases without naming the flashlight trick, and a stuck player escalates from per-death narrator whispers to the explicit hintText() ("Switch on the phone's flashlight. Stand the phone upright to throw the beam far ahead — tilt it flat to pool the light...") after struggleCount>=2 and >=8s.
reviews.suggestion: Make the discovery window shorter and self-serve: if the flashlight is still OFF after ~6-8s of no progress (not gated behind 2 deaths), surface a soft mid-tier nudge that points at the device's flashlight control without the full solution — e.g. animate/pulse the on-screen flashlight HUD button. Separately, raise the iPad silhouette-hint alpha to ~0.10-0.12 so the dark reads as an intentional cave rather than a void even before the beam is on.

reviews.mechanic: Multi-touch: hold every screen-space contact pad in a group simultaneously (2, then 3, then 4) to drop the matching gate, then walk Bit through while still holding.
reviews.strengths: Numbered gate badges (2/3/4) plus the now-readable 0.2-alpha circuit lines clearly map each plate cluster to its gate, so once the player grasps the trick the "how many fingers" escalation is legible and well-juiced. Reachability is airtight: gates and exit live on one continuous ground with no pits or hazards, so the level is always completable by walking and holding, never climb-bypassable.
reviews.concerns: The core conceptual leap (pads are HUD fingers you HOLD, not surfaces Bit walks onto) is never stated until the hint fires, so first-timers may try to platform Bit onto the pads. On iPad the hand-composed climb is purely decorative vertical-fill that the player never needs to ascend, yet it visually invites climbing toward the high plates, and rungs crowd the plate rings in places.
reviews.clueHint: t=0 "ONE OF YOU IS NOT ENOUGH." / "CONTACT REQUIRED" hints at multiplicity without spoiling the finger trick. Escalation exists (notePlayerStruggle on death, whispers at 2/3/4 touches, full reveal in hintText) but is death-gated, and this level has no hazards or pits, so a confused-but-alive player may never trigger it.
reviews.suggestion: Add an inactivity/no-progress timer (not just death) to fire notePlayerStruggle, and make the first whisper explicitly say "use more than one finger" so a stuck-but-alive player gets escalating help; on iPad, either gate the climb to actual traversal or visually de-emphasize it so it stops reading as a required platforming route.

reviews.mechanic: Flat single-screen finale: walk right through two self-opening joke gates to a padlocked EXIT; the review prompt padlock auto-breaks after 10s whether or not you tap "VALIDATE ME."
reviews.strengths: The gag lands: t=0 promises "JUST GET TO THE EXIT" while the real puzzle is the fourth-wall review ask, and the level can never soft-lock because the padlock auto-breaks after 10s. Completability is bulletproof on iPhone: it is a pure flat walk with zero jumps, gaps, or rises, and the gates open on contact rather than gating behind a jump.
reviews.concerns: iPad framing is the main issue: gameplayLift centers the band but leaves the entire lower ~40% of the canvas as empty dotted void (decor only brackets the upper band, nothing fills below the ground line). The gates also lose legibility on iPad, reading as thin full-height white poles fused with the new pillars rather than two distinct gate uprights, so the "gate" gag is much weaker than on iPhone.
reviews.clueHint: Good: the t=0 panel ("HOW HARD CAN IT BE?") sets up the joke without exposing it, and hintText() escalates to "The game wants something from you..." pointing at the review ask without spelling it out. But there is no notePlayerStruggle hook, and the 10s auto-unlock makes the hint nearly moot since a stuck player is rescued automatically.
reviews.suggestion: On iPad, anchor the flat band lower (reduce gameplayLift so the ground sits near the lower third) and/or extend the decorative shaft structure below the ground line, so the empty bottom void is eliminated; while there, give the two gates a distinct cap/base so they don't visually merge with the new vertical pillars.
